---
title: "Executable Specifications and Lean Proof Envelope"
chapter_id: "executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope"
part_id: "routing-compression-evidence-implementation"
status: "conceptual"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "genesiscode"
  - "spinoza"
  - "scf"
  - "talos"
  - "viea"
evidence_level: "argument"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Assigned source texts have not yet been ingested or summarized."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run."
  - "Chapter prose is a scaffold, not a completed manuscript draft."
---

# Executable Specifications and Lean Proof Envelope

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Evidence, and Implementation |
| Status | conceptual |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `genesiscode`, `spinoza`, `scf`, `talos`, `viea` |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source ingestion state | Source records assigned; source texts not yet ingested. |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run. |

## Drafting guardrail

This stub is derived from `book_structure.json` and the source inventory only. It does not claim that the listed source documents have been ingested, summarized, or independently verified.

## Problem

The book needs to decide which architecture claims should become executable specs or Lean proofs.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Formal methods are ineffective when applied to vague philosophical claims instead of operational predicates and transitions.

## Core claim

[executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope.core, support: argument] Lean proofs and executable specs should target small invariants: authority, support states, SCF replacement, job lifecycle, context adequacy, and residual records.

## Mechanism

- Translate mechanisms into predicates and transition rules.
- Keep proof manifest generated from the outline.
- Distinguish scaffolded, implemented, blocked, and retired proof targets.

## Interfaces

- Outline defines proof scope.
- Lean modules implement selected invariants.
- Validation checks proof-manifest consistency.

## Invariants

- No theorem is claimed proven without `lake build`.
- Proof tags remain stable.
- Broad claims are decomposed before formalization.

## Failure modes

- Formalization theater.
- Unstable proof target names.
- Claiming proof from prose.

## Minimal implementation

A proof manifest, Lean workspace, and first invariant modules for support-state and authority checks.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Proof manifest sync test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |
| Lake build smoke test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |
| Proof target coverage audit | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Planned use |
|---|---|---|
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. |
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | Planned use from inventory/manifest: AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. |

## Summary

This chapter currently establishes the structural slot for this layer of the ASI stack. The next drafting pass should ingest assigned sources, separate source-derived claims from design hypotheses, and update Appendix C when support states change.
